We're not going to spend any time on it, but page 49 through, I don't know, page 70 is a chapter out of a book about the creation of superheroes. And the one thing I would point out to you is that there's a formula here, kind of as a piece of trivia. The formula is enunciated by Judge Lernan Hand, who is a Supreme Court Justice most famous for his remarks about your requirement to pay taxes, but he actually decided the first lawsuit in the comic book industry. And in doing so, wrote this definition of what a superhero is. And the main component parts are there has to be a mission. So people want you to be doing things for reasons other than purely your self-interest, economic interest. Doesn't mean that you can't disclose that what you do is for economic interest and self-interest. In fact, it's usually useful to do so because it's an elephant in the room. But they want you to have another reason. They want you to be, see, like the language whoever gave that to you at the table, the hero of small business owners. See, that's really good language because it gets to mission. And there's a good origin story for you for that position and so forth. So that's leverageable. Next, of course, superheroes have to have superpowers. However, they can be, in the judge's description, they can merely be mystical skills. So if you think about it, by the way, from the comic book industry, some of the most enduring superheroes actually have no superpowers at all. So Superman has superpowers. Batman has not. But Batman has, everybody's popular, Superman. It has endured roughly the same period of time. So you either have to have superpowers or you have to have mystical skills. Obviously, I'm talking about how you're perceived, not as you really are, because of course none of us have either one of those things. But we can be perceived as if we have them. You have to have an origin story. There has to be a reason you are where you are doing what you are doing. And so when you go to build a legend, you're really building a su-fictional, legendary, superhero character, an extension of yourself. So your diagnostic abilities with hydraulic papoos have to rise to the mystical level. And stories of being able to, there's a, in the racehorse industry, there's a person who makes actually a sizable amount of money and she's a horse psychic. And so people pay her to come and talk to the horse and figure out why the horse is not racing well. And they fly her all over the country, by the way. So apparently a couple of years ago she got tired of travel. So she is added now to her legend. She literally can, you can take the phone into the stall and leave it there. And she is able to detect what is bothering the horse from a distance. Now you want to talk about an improbable claim. This is it. However, people with horses, they can't figure out why the hell they're not doing well, are very eager to believe this particular story. And now there are legendary stories of her having been on the phone with the horse for 30 minutes and telling the owner he really, he hates the stall he's in and needs to be moved to one facing the east with a window. And he doesn't like the person who's taking care of him and they make those changes and the horse comes to life and wins the Kentucky Derby. Well, you only need a couple of those stories and you're done, right? You got it. So she has now mystical powers. Nobody can imagine how the hell she does this. And everybody is cheerfully willing to give her money. So those are the key component parts that you want to build in. The rest of the article you can read on your own, but it is instructive. And if it really interests you, the whole book is instructive. The making of a superhero. Every superhero has fallibility. Otherwise, people lose all interest in them. And so the most super of all superheroes is Superman, for which they had to create the Krypton story after about six months because people were losing interest in them. So and of course the Clark Kent side of that story, you know, is somebody who is unsuccessful with women, bad social skills, frustrated, etc. The Marvel universe of comic heroes is even more sort of emotionally dysfunctional. So the Spider-Man characters far more screwed up than the DC Comics characters. One does not preclude, you know, the other. So that part of the super heroes is there's a flaw? Mm-hmm. Well, because people can't identify. See people can't, it's like his problem with all of his stories getting too late in his game. So now the customer can't identify with where he is now. So you have to have connection, right? So Spider-Man, hugely popular with teenage kids, first of all the alter ego is a teenager and with all of the same, the Spider-Man character was actually written as Archie bitten by a spider. So it's all of the same problems Archie has with women and school and all of that. And now just he gets bitten by a spider so he has spider powers and he gets a costume. Both characters exist in the same body in the same story. Most of the characters in the early days of the comic book industry, the ones they created that were truly flawless, that were totally super disappeared, could not hold an audience. And so we're always showing both sides. See people want to believe that we are extremely superior to be looked up to, right? So from a financial standpoint they like to think we have the Midas touch and everything we touch turns into money. And that, you know, we have to show them our affluence in one way or another that they have to aspire to, etc., etc., etc. If Bill decides to be a management guru, they actually have to believe that he has mystical powers for selecting employees who turn out to be great employees, etc. But on the other hand, there's got to be some flaws and some pain and some angst that I can connect with, right? So we've got to bring both things to the table. Principle number five on page 65 is pretty interesting. That's the thing a lot of people forget. And it's called the principle of prevention. And that is about making sure that you don't leave room for things about you to take root in whatever your public discussion is, your audience discussion is, that don't serve you well. And so, A, you've got to give thought to what those might be and preemptively act on them. And if they do occur, you have to aggressively act to correct them. So like one of the things I've always been very, very careful about is, and the further away from business and the more I got to be known as, see like the worst thing for me was being known as a speaker. Because that's the least connected to the audience you could possibly be. The worst or worse of that is a motivational speaker because that carries all kinds of bullshit connotations too. But that's the worst thing to be known for. So speaker author doesn't serve me well if that becomes the main thing because I'm talking to business people. So I have always been very, very careful to beat the drum of serial entrepreneur in the trenches just like you are. I get up every day, my whole little Peter Lowe stump speech was kind of started with close to saying that everybody you've heard before me during the day sells only in their memories not in reality, hasn't run a business ever or in six million years. But I'm coming to you from current on the street getting my brains beat out every day like you experience. And so because what I've always wanted to prevent is the position that guys like Gerber and Brian and a lot of guys find themselves in where there's no current connection to the audience. So yeah, he used to sell real estate but he used to do it 25 years ago. What the hell does he know about what I'm going through in my market? And so I always wanted to prevent that getting into their minds. We have a...
